question: Explain the principle of natural circulation cooling in the NuScale design.

nuscale_ch05_0025.title: References
nuscale_ch05_0025.summary: This section details the materials used in the construction of the pressurizer and its components within the NuScale reactor coolant system. It specifies the types of steel and alloys used for various parts, their compliance with relevant BPVC material specifications, and highlights corrosion protection measures such as weld cladding with stainless steel and nickel-based alloys.

nuscale_ch05_0002.title: Summary Description
nuscale_ch05_0002.summary: The NuScale reactor coolant system (RCS) uses natural circulation without pumps or external piping to transfer heat from the reactor core to the steam generator, ensuring safe and efficient operation. The RCS design supports up to twelve independent modules, maintains proper coolant chemistry and boron concentration, and is engineered to prevent fuel design limit violations during all operating conditions.

nuscale_ch05_0023.title: Reactor Coolant System High-Point Vents
nuscale_ch05_0023.summary: This section describes the testing and inspection requirements for the Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) components, including compliance with ASME codes for preservice and inservice inspections. It explains that DHRS actuation valves are exercised during cold shutdown conditions, and outlines the procedures for valve testing, position verification, and a one-time in-situ test of DHRS heat removal capability during initial reactor module startup.

nuscale_ch05_0017.title: Reactor Vessel Integrity
nuscale_ch05_0017.summary: This section outlines the procedures and regulatory compliance measures for maintaining the integrity of the NuScale reactor vessel. It details the development of operating procedures to ensure pressure-temperature limits are not exceeded, evaluates the vessel’s resistance to pressurized thermal shock and upper-shelf energy degradation over a 60-year design life, and confirms that all criteria remain within regulatory limits.

nuscale_ch05_0003.title: Design Basis
nuscale_ch05_0003.summary: The Design Basis section outlines that the NuScale reactor coolant system (RCS) uses natural circulation without pumps or external piping, ensuring safe and efficient heat transfer from the reactor core to the steam generator. The RCS is designed to maintain fuel integrity, minimize solid deposits, and ensure uniform boron concentration under all operating conditions, supporting both performance and safety requirements.

nuscale_ch05_0016.title: Pressure-Temperature Limits, Pressurized Thermal Shock, and Charpy Upper-Shelf Energy Data and Analyses
nuscale_ch05_0016.summary: This section outlines the basis for establishing operational pressure and temperature limits for the reactor coolant pressure boundary (RCPB) to ensure compliance with regulatory requirements over the plant’s 60-year lifespan. It describes the methodology used to calculate these limits, accounting for vessel embrittlement due to neutron exposure, and provides the resulting pressure-temperature curves and values for various operational conditions to prevent non-ductile failure of the reactor vessel.

nuscale_ch05_0004.title: System Description
nuscale_ch05_0004.summary: The Reactor Coolant System (RCS) in the NuScale design is located inside the containment vessel and includes key components such as the reactor pressure vessel, pressurizer, and associated piping and controls. The RCS removes heat from the reactor core by natural circulation, using differences in coolant density and elevation to drive flow, and interfaces with several other systems for safety and operational functions, including degasification and emergency cooling.

edges.description: Section B explains that the NuScale design relies on natural circulation flow for reactor coolant, while Section A details the pressurizer components that are part of the reactor coolant system described in Section B.

Section A describes the materials and components of the pressurizer within the reactor coolant system, while Section B outlines inspection and classification requirements for components (including valves) of the decay heat removal system (DHRS), both of which are subsystems integral to the overall reactor coolant system design in the NuScale plant.

Section A describes the materials and components of the pressurizer, which is part of the reactor coolant system, while Section B discusses the development of operating procedures to ensure reactor vessel integrity by maintaining pressure-temperature limits, thus both sections are connected through their focus on maintaining safe pressure and temperature conditions in the reactor coolant system.

Section B explains that the NuScale design relies on natural circulation flow for reactor coolant, while Section A details the pressurizer components that are part of the reactor coolant system described in Section B.

Section A explains that the NuScale design relies on natural circulation for reactor coolant flow, while Section B discusses inspection requirements for components (like DHRS actuation valves) that support the safe operation of systems relying on natural circulation, thus Section B provides supporting operational and safety context for the principle described in Section A.

Section A explains that the NuScale design uses natural circulation for reactor coolant flow, which directly impacts the operating procedures in Section B that are developed to ensure pressure-temperature limits are maintained under this passive cooling method.

Section A discusses inspection and classification requirements for DHRS components, which are part of the natural circulation cooling system, while Section B addresses the development of operating procedures to ensure reactor vessel integrity under various conditions, both of which are necessary to ensure the safe and effective operation of natural circulation cooling in the NuScale design.

Section B explains that the NuScale design relies on natural circulation flow for reactor coolant, while Section A discusses inspection requirements for components (like DHRS valves) that support this cooling principle, thus Section A provides supporting operational and safety details for the general natural circulation principle described in Section B.

Section B explains that the NuScale design relies on natural circulation flow for reactor coolant, which is the principle underlying the operating procedures in Section A that ensure reactor vessel integrity by maintaining appropriate pressure-temperature limits.

Section B provides references to material descriptions and component details that are relevant to the reactor vessel discussed in Section A, connecting the structural and material aspects of the NuScale design.

Section A provides a general system-level description of the reactor coolant system's degasification and pressure boundary functions, while Section B specifies the detailed component materials and construction of the pressurizer, which is part of the system described in Section A.

Section B explains that the NuScale design relies on natural circulation for reactor coolant flow, while Section A provides specific details about system components (like degasification lines and vent valves) that support the operation and integrity of the reactor coolant system described in Section B.

Section A describes the system's capability to remove noncondensible gases via venting, which is a prerequisite for effective natural circulation cooling, while Section B details the inspection and classification requirements for the valves involved in this process.

Section A discusses material and weld examination requirements for the reactor vessel, while Section B describes the reactor coolant system's function in maintaining pressure boundary integrity; both sections are connected through their focus on ensuring the structural integrity and safe operation of the reactor vessel, which is essential for natural circulation cooling in the NuScale design.
